Git.keywords: git, vcs, gitignore, branches, commits, hooks, history, tags, secrets, repo-bootstrap

Directive_Context.Mission:
Define foundational git policy for every project: gitignore baseline, branch strategy, commit convention, history hygiene, tag/release semantics, hooks integration, secrets discipline, and repo bootstrap.
Goal: a repo that is reproducible, auditable, and safe — no accidental secret leaks, no platform-specific binaries committed, no fabricated «green» status hiding broken history.
Scope is git itself. Tool choice for hooks runner (lefthook / husky / simple-git-hooks) is the `git-hooks` category in `infra-discovery`, distinct from this rule. CI provider configuration is the `ci` category, also distinct.
Directive_Context.Functional_Perspective: You are a repository steward. Reproducibility, auditability, safety — in that order. The repo is the project's canonical record; every commit becomes part of the audit trail. Lock-file commits and exact `.gitignore` rules are not pedantry — they are the contract that makes the project survive contributor turnover and CI/local environment drift.

Belief_State.Axiom:
**`.gitignore` covers three layers: mandatory, tool-derived, environment.**
Mandatory (every project): - `.claude/settings.local.json` — personal Claude Code overrides; team-shared `.claude/settings.json` IS tracked. - `.env`, `.env.local`, `.env.*.local` — secrets; only `.env.example` (no real values) is tracked. - OS artifacts: `.DS_Store`, `Thumbs.db`, `desktop.ini`. - Editor caches: `.idea/`, `*.swp`, `.vscode/` (or selectively, project-team decision). - Coverage outputs: `coverage/`, `*.lcov`, `.nyc_output/`. - Logs: `*.log`, `npm-debug.log*`, `yarn-error.log`, etc.
Tool-derived (per chosen stack — derived from `infra-discovery` choices): - Node ecosystem: `node_modules/`, `dist/`, `.next/`, `.nuxt/`, `.cache/`. - Python: `__pycache__/`, `*.pyc`, `.venv/`, `.pytest_cache/`, `.ruff_cache/`. - Go: `vendor/` (if not committed by policy), build artifacts. - Rust: `target/`.
Environment-specific (allow developer-local additions): - Personal scripts under `scripts/local/` — developer-team decision.
**Why:** `.gitignore` is the first contract every contributor encounters. Wrong here = secrets in repo, multi-GB bloat, platform-specific native binaries breaking CI.

**Pick one branch strategy at project init, document in README, enforce via remote branch protection.**
Three accepted variants: - **trunk-based** — short-lived branches off `main`, merged within a day. Default for small teams + strong CI. (Recommended default.) - **github-flow** — long-lived feature branches, PR-driven, no `develop` branch. - **git-flow** — `main` + `develop` + `release/*` + `hotfix/*`. For projects with planned release cadences.
Default branch name: `main` (not `master`). Set both locally and on remote.
Branch naming convention: `/`, where `type ∈ {feature, fix, chore, docs, refactor, test, perf}`. Lowercase, kebab-case.
**Why:** Mixing strategies within one team multiplies merge confusion. Pick one, document it, enforce it.

**Default: Conventional Commits format (`(): `). Alternatives acceptable with documented rationale.**
Conventional Commits format: - Types: `feat`, `fix`, `docs`, `chore`, `refactor`, `test`, `perf`, `build`, `ci`, `style`, `revert`. - Subject: imperative mood, ≤72 chars, no trailing period. - Body (optional): wraps at 100 chars, blank line between subject and body. - Footer: `BREAKING CHANGE: ...`, issue refs, sign-off.
Example: `feat(auth): add OAuth2 PKCE flow for native clients`.
Acceptable alternatives (operator choice with rationale in Decision Log): - **plain** — no type prefix, just imperative subject. Lower ceremony, no semantic structure. - **DCO sign-off** — `Signed-off-by: Name ` mandatory for licensing/legal. Compatible with conventional commits.
**Why:** Conventional Commits enables auto-changelog, semantic-release, and machine-readable git history. The discipline cost is paid once; the visibility benefit accrues forever.

**Linear history via rebase or squash-merge for PRs. Force-push forbidden on shared branches.**
Shared branches (force-push FORBIDDEN): `main`, `develop`, `release/*`, any branch with active PRs from others. Personal branches (force-push allowed): your own `feature/*`, `fix/*` until merged.
Merge strategy options: - **squash-merge** — feature branch becomes single commit on main. Simple history; loses commit granularity. - **rebase-and-merge** — feature commits rebased onto main, no merge commit. Preserves granularity; requires clean local history. - **merge commit** — discouraged unless you specifically want the branch shape preserved (e.g., release branches).
Hooks bypass: `--no-verify` is FORBIDDEN unless operator explicitly authorizes; if used, the bypass MUST be documented in the commit body.
**Why:** Shared-branch force-push silently rewrites history collaborators have already pulled. Recovery is manual and error-prone. Linear history makes `git bisect` and code archaeology possible.

**Destructive git commands risk silent data loss. They are NOT blanket forbidden — they're tools — but each invocation requires explicit operator authorization recorded in the Execution Log.**
**Requires explicit auth (status `[!] BLOCKED` until operator confirms):** - `git reset --hard ` — discards working tree + index changes since ``. - `git restore .` (without specific path) — discards all uncommitted changes in working tree. - `git restore --staged --worktree ` with broad path — same risk. - `git clean -fd` / `-fdx` — deletes untracked files (and ignored-files with `-x`). - `git checkout -- ` (or `git checkout HEAD -- ` with broad path) — discards uncommitted changes. - `git push --force` / `--force-with-lease` on shared branches (`main`, `develop`, `release/*`). - `git branch -D ` (force-delete unmerged) — distinct from `-d` (safe delete). - `git rebase -i` — interactive history rewrite. - `git reflog expire` / `git gc --prune=now` — drops recoverable history. - `git filter-repo` / `git filter-branch` — bulk history rewrite.
**Required protocol per invocation:** 1. State the intent in Execution Log: «Need to ; proposing .» 2. Set status `[!] BLOCKED` per `AX_BLOCKER_ESCALATION` (in `task-execution`). 3. Wait for operator's explicit confirmation. 4. Only then run.
**Recovery first, destruction second.** When something looks broken, the reflex «let me reset --hard and start over» is the failure mode this axiom exists to prevent. Recovery via: - `git reflog` — find the lost commit/state. - `git stash` — park uncommitted changes safely. - `git revert` — create an inverse commit (preserves history). - `git checkout -- ` — restore one file from a known-good commit.
Recovery preserves data; destruction loses it. Default to recovery.
**The operator-confirmed exception path:** if the operator explicitly says «yes, reset hard, that's exactly what I want» — fine. The auth record in Execution Log is the audit trail. The agent never assumes the answer is yes.
**Forbidden as habit:** - «Let me just reset --hard and try again» — recovery is the operator's call. - `git clean -fdx` to «free up disk» without listing what will be deleted. - `--force-with-lease` on `main` because it «sounds safer than --force» — both are destructive on shared branches.

**Semver tags: `vMAJOR.MINOR.PATCH`. Annotated tags only.**
Format: - Stable: `v1.2.3`. - Pre-release: `v1.2.3-alpha.1`, `v1.2.3-beta.2`, `v1.2.3-rc.1`. - Build metadata (optional): `v1.2.3+20260507` — discouraged; use only when build-system requires.
Always annotated: ``` git tag -a v1.2.3 -m "Release 1.2.3 — " ``` Lightweight tags (`git tag v1.2.3` without `-a`) are FORBIDDEN — they carry no metadata and look identical to branch refs.
Tags pushed explicitly: `git push origin v1.2.3` (or `--tags` after release).
**Why:** Annotated tags carry tagger identity, date, and message; they are first-class objects. Lightweight tags are mistakes waiting to surface.

**Hooks fire on every commit and push; use the runner chosen in `git-hooks` category.**
Required hooks: - **pre-commit** — lint changed files, format check, type-check (if applicable). Should complete in <5s for typical change. Failures BLOCK the commit. - **commit-msg** — conventional-commits validator if `AX_COMMIT_CONVENTION = conventional`. - **pre-push** — full test suite (unit + integration if fast). Failures BLOCK the push.
Optional hooks: - **prepare-commit-msg** — auto-prepend issue ID from branch name. - **post-merge** — run `npm install` / `pnpm install` if lock-file changed.
Hooks are checked into the repo (via lefthook.yml / husky scripts / etc.); developer onboarding includes one command to register them (e.g., `pnpm exec lefthook install`).
**Why:** Hooks catch trivial mistakes locally before CI burns minutes. They also document team norms in code rather than wiki.

**LFS for large binaries. Submodules discouraged.**
Git LFS: - Required when tracking binary files >100MB (GitHub hard limit; other hosts vary). - Configure `.gitattributes` to mark patterns: `*.psd filter=lfs diff=lfs merge=lfs -text`. - LFS adds infrastructure cost (storage, bandwidth quota) — use only when binaries are necessary.
Submodules: - Discouraged in favor of monorepo structure or package dependencies. - If unavoidable: pin to specific commit (not branch); document in README why submodule was chosen. - Submodule update workflow is fragile: `git submodule update --init --recursive` after every checkout.
**Why:** LFS solves the «huge binary» problem without bloating the main repo. Submodules solve the «cross-repo dependency» problem but introduce checkout fragility — usually a package manager or monorepo handles this better.

**First commit only after `.gitignore` is in place. Remote protection enforced before first push.**
Bootstrap sequence: 1. `git init` (or `git init -b main` to set default branch immediately). 2. Author `.gitignore` with at minimum the mandatory layer from `AX_GITIGNORE_BASELINE`. Tool-derived ignores added as `infra-discovery` decisions resolve. 3. First commit: foundational files only — `LICENSE`, `README.md`, `.gitignore`. Message: `chore: initial commit`. 4. Add remote and first push only AFTER `.gitignore` is verified — protects against accidental secret commits. 5. Configure remote branch protection on `main`: - Require PR before merge (no direct push). - Require status checks (CI green) before merge. - Disallow force-push.
**Why:** First push is the riskiest moment for secret leaks — `.env` files might exist locally and accidentally enter the index. `.gitignore` first prevents this class of mistake.

**Secrets never committed. On leak: rotate first, rewrite history second.**
Prevention: - `.env`, `.env.local`, `.env.*.local` always gitignored (`AX_GITIGNORE_BASELINE`). - `.env.example` tracked with placeholder values to document required keys. - Pre-commit hook scans staged files for secret patterns (API keys, private keys, AWS access keys) — recommended tools: `gitleaks`, `detect-secrets`, `trufflehog`. - Code review checks for hardcoded credentials in any file extension (not just `.env`).
On leak detection: 1. **Rotate the leaked credential immediately** (revoke + reissue). The credential is compromised the moment it hits a remote, regardless of subsequent rewrite. 2. Document the incident: what leaked, when, who rotated, what notifications went out. 3. Optional: `git filter-repo` or BFG to scrub history, then force-push (coordinate with team).
**Why:** Git history is forever. Once a secret reaches the remote, assume it's harvested by scanners within minutes. Rotation is mandatory; history rewrite is hygiene, not security.

Anti_Pattern.Why_Bad: 10–500 MB bloat; macOS-compiled native binaries fail on Linux CI; PR diffs become unreadable. Lock file already encodes the dep tree deterministically.
Anti_Pattern.Good: `node_modules/` in `.gitignore`. `npm ci` / `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` restores from lock file.

Anti_Pattern.Why_Bad: Silently rewrites history collaborators already pulled. Recovery requires every team member to reset their local branch — error-prone, sometimes impossible if uncommitted work is on top.
Anti_Pattern.Good: Force-push only on personal branches. Shared-branch issues fixed via revert commits.

Anti_Pattern.Bad: Committed `.env` with real API key, then `git rm` in next commit.
Anti_Pattern.Why_Bad: The secret is in the history. Anyone can `git log -p` and recover it. Public repos expose to scanners within seconds.
Anti_Pattern.Good: Rotate the credential immediately. Then rewrite history with `git filter-repo` and force-push (coordinate with team). Add `.env` to `.gitignore` BEFORE first push.

Anti_Pattern.Bad: `git commit --no-verify` because pre-commit hook is «too slow».
Anti_Pattern.Why_Bad: Hooks exist to catch mistakes locally. Routinely bypassing them shifts those mistakes into CI (slower, more expensive) or main (worse). If hooks are too slow, fix the hooks — don't skip them.
Anti_Pattern.Good: Diagnose hook slowness (lint scope too wide? full test suite in pre-commit instead of pre-push?). Optimize the hook. `--no-verify` only with explicit operator authorization, documented in commit body.

Anti_Pattern.Why_Bad: Lightweight tag has no tagger / date / message metadata. It looks identical to a branch ref in some tools. Release lineage is unrecoverable.
Anti_Pattern.Good: `git tag -a v1.2.3 -m "Release 1.2.3 — "`. Always annotated for release tags.

Hook.Command: git ls-files | grep -E '^\.env($|\.)' | grep -v '^\.env\.example$' | head -1 | grep -q . && echo "FAIL: .env tracked" || echo "OK"
git ls-files node_modules 2>/dev/null | head -1 | grep -q . && echo "FAIL: node_modules tracked" || echo "OK"
git symbolic-ref refs/remotes/origin/HEAD 2>/dev/null | grep -q 'main$' && echo "OK" || echo "FAIL: default branch not main"

Hook.Purpose: Verify all release tags are annotated (object type = tag, not commit).
Hook.Command: for t in $(git tag); do test "$(git cat-file -t $t)" = "tag" || echo "FAIL: $t is lightweight"; done; echo "OK"

Git.Reward_Criteria:
✅ REWARD: `.gitignore` covers mandatory layer (`.claude/settings.local.json`, `.env*`, OS / editor / coverage / log artifacts); tool-derived layer matches chosen stack. ✅ REWARD: Default branch is `main`; remote branch protection prevents direct push, requires PR + green checks. ✅ REWARD: Branch strategy declared in README, consistently applied. ✅ REWARD: Commit messages follow chosen convention (default Conventional Commits); subjects ≤72 chars, imperative mood. ✅ REWARD: History on shared branches is linear (rebase or squash-merge); no force-push on `main` / `develop` / `release/*`. ✅ REWARD: Release tags annotated, semver format `vMAJOR.MINOR.PATCH`. ✅ REWARD: Hooks runner installed via single onboarding command; pre-commit + commit-msg + pre-push hooks present. ✅ REWARD: No `.env*` (except `.env.example`) tracked; pre-commit secret scanner in place. ✅ REWARD: First commit happens AFTER `.gitignore` is in place; first push happens AFTER remote branch protection is configured.
❌ PENALTY: Secrets in history (any `.env` with real values, hardcoded API keys, private keys). ❌ PENALTY: `node_modules/` (or equivalent) committed. ❌ PENALTY: Force-push to `main` / `develop` / shared release branches. ❌ PENALTY: Lightweight tags (`git tag v1.2.3` without `-a`) used for releases. ❌ PENALTY: `--no-verify` used routinely without operator authorization. ✅ REWARD: Destructive commands (`reset --hard`, `restore .`, `clean -fd`, force-push, etc.) preceded by explicit operator authorization recorded in Execution Log; recovery preferred over destruction. ❌ PENALTY: Destructive git command run as «let me just reset --hard and try again» reflex — without operator authorization or attempt at recovery via reflog/stash/revert. ❌ PENALTY: Default branch named `master` in new project. ❌ PENALTY: `.claude/settings.local.json` tracked (personal overrides leak).
